owaspCategoriesReviewed: CRM-specific: multi-tenant isolation (ADR-004/ADR-009/ADR-025), audit hash-chain integrity (ADR-008/ADR-056), webhook signature verification, AI output/prompt handling
meta.summary: 9 findings recorded. The headline result is that the multi-tenancy schema migration described as 'Proposed' in ADR-025 is actually complete in packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma (tenantId present + indexed on LeadActivity, ContactActivity, DealProduct, AgentAction, Document, MessageRecord, etc.), and ADR-056's audit hash-chain race is already fixed in code. However, two systemic gaps undercut the RLS-as-backstop model that ADR-004/ADR-009 rely on: (1) none of the 69 'ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY' statements in the baseline migration are paired with 'FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY', so if the Prisma connection role owns the tables (the normal case for a migration-running DATABASE_URL role) RLS is bypassed entirely for that connection, making application-layer tenantId filtering the ONLY real control; and (2) at least one tenant-scoped procedure (contact.searchContacts in the inbound-email router) queries via the raw (unscoped) ctx.prisma client with no tenantId in its where-clause at all, which is a concrete, reproducible cross-tenant IDOR given gap (1). A second access-control gap was found where a router's own docstrings claim 'admin only' but the code uses protectedProcedure (any authenticated role) instead of the project's own adminProcedure. A third finding shows the inbound-email webhook is a fully public, unauthenticated tRPC mutation that persists forged 'DELIVERED' emails into a resolved tenant's inbox with no shared-secret/signature check.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `searchContacts: tenantProcedure.input(z.object({ query: z.string(), limit: z.number().default(5) })).query(async ({ input, ctx }) => { ... const contacts = await (ctx as any).prisma.contact.findMany({ where: { OR: [ { firstName: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } }, { lastName: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } }, { email: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } } ] }, select: { id, firstName, lastName, email, company }, take: input.limit, orderBy: { firstName: 'asc' } }); return contacts; })` at apps/api/src/modules/email/inbound.router.ts:1179-1216. `where` contains only an `OR` array — no `tenantId` key anywhere — and the query runs on the raw `(ctx as any).prisma` client (not `ctx.prismaWithTenant`, which is the only client that sets the RLS session variable — see apps/api/src/trpc.ts:291-327 and apps/api/src/security/tenant-context.ts:119-149). Every sibling procedure in the same router (getEmail L274-280, listEmails L297-298, processEmail L365-369, getThread L503-521, getAttachment L567-573, lookupByEmail L992-997/1026-1027, listTemplates L793-794, markAsRead/markAsUnread L827/853, setLabels L886-891, getStorageUsage L1071, getRelatedMessages L1138-1144) explicitly threads `tenantId` into `where`, confirming this is the one procedure where it was omitted, not an intentional global-search feature.
findings.impact: Any authenticated user of ANY tenant (auth only requires `tenantProcedure`, i.e. a valid JWT + tenantId claim — no special role) can enumerate contact firstName/lastName/email/company belonging to every OTHER tenant in the system via the email-compose recipient autocomplete. Direct violation of ADR-004's 'absolute guarantee that one tenant cannot access another's data' decision driver and ADR-009's zero-trust model. Because RLS is not FORCE-enabled on `contacts` (see SEC-002), there is no database-level backstop once the app-layer filter is missing — this is a fully exploitable data leak, not merely a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.rootCause: Developer copy-pasted a generic contact-search query without the tenantId filter that every other procedure in the same file applies; no lint/type-level guard (e.g. a branded `TenantScopedWhere` type or an ESLint rule) forces `tenantId` into Prisma `where` clauses for tenant-scoped models.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-004-multi-tenancy.md (row-level isolation guarantee), ADR-009-zero-trust-security.md (Multi-Tenant Isolation hard requirement), ADR-025-tenant-id-normalization.md
findings.remediation: Add `tenantId` to the `where` clause (`where: { tenantId, OR: [...] }`) using `(ctx as any).tenant.tenantId` exactly as the other procedures in this file do, OR switch the query to `ctx.prismaWithTenant.contact.findMany(...)` so RLS session context is also set as a second layer. Add a regression test asserting cross-tenant contacts are never returned. Recommend a repo-wide grep audit (`ctx.prisma.<model>.find*` without `tenantId` in the same call) as a follow-up task, and consider an ESLint rule or Prisma extension that fails the build on tenant-model queries missing tenantId.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -c "ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/20260317000000_baseline/migration.sql` → 69. `grep -c "FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/20260317000000_baseline/migration.sql` → 0 (confirmed repo-wide: `grep -rc "FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/*/migration.sql` returns 0 for every migration file). Line 4822: `ALTER TABLE "contacts" ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` with policy at line 4832 `CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_contacts ON "contacts" FOR ALL USING ("tenantId" = get_current_tenant_id());` — never followed by `ALTER TABLE "contacts" FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` anywhere in the migrations tree. Per PostgreSQL semantics, a table's OWNER role is exempt from its own RLS policies unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is also set; the Prisma client connects via a single `DATABASE_URL` role (packages/db/src/client.ts:125: `connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL!`) that is the same role which ran these migrations (i.e. very likely the table owner in a standard Supabase/Prisma setup).
findings.impact: If (as is standard) the app's DATABASE_URL role owns the tables it migrated, RLS silently does not apply to ANY query issued over that connection — including `ctx.prismaWithTenant` queries that intentionally `SET app.current_tenant_id`. RLS then provides zero defense-in-depth despite ADR-004/ADR-009 explicitly selling it as 'preventing tenant data leakage even if application code has bugs' and 'database-enforced isolation ... even if application is compromised'. This directly amplifies the blast radius of SEC-001 and of any future missing-tenantId bug from 'contained by RLS' to 'fully exploitable'.
findings.rootCause: RLS policies were authored assuming a non-owner application role (the textbook Supabase pattern: `anon`/`authenticated` roles queried through PostgREST are never table owners), but this codebase connects Prisma directly with one connection string used both for migrations and application queries, so the owner-bypass exemption applies unless FORCE is added.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-004-multi-tenancy.md ('Database-enforced isolation: RLS policies prevent tenant data leakage even if application code has bugs'), ADR-009-zero-trust-security.md ('RLS prevents unauthorized data access ... even if application is compromised')
findings.remediation: Verify which Postgres role `DATABASE_URL` connects as in each environment and whether it owns the migrated tables (`SELECT tableowner FROM pg_tables WHERE tablename='contacts';` vs the connection's `current_user`). If it is the owner (expected), add `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` for every tenant-scoped table in a follow-up migration, or provision a separate non-owner application role for query traffic (migrations continue to run as the owner/admin role). Add an integration test that connects as the actual app role and asserts a query without `app.current_tenant_id` set returns zero rows across tenants.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: Confidence is capped because the actual Postgres role identity/ownership in the deployed Supabase project was not directly inspected (this is a read-only, no-DB-access audit) — the finding is a strong architectural inference from migration SQL + connection code, not a confirmed live exploit. If the deployment already uses a distinct non-owner role for DATABASE_URL, this finding does not apply and should be downgraded to Info.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 112-140: `/** Register a new webhook source * Protected endpoint - admin only */ registerSource: protectedProcedure.input(registerSourceSchema).mutation(...)` and lines 142-160 `/** Unregister a webhook source * Protected endpoint - admin only */ unregisterSource: protectedProcedure.input(...).mutation(...)`. Both use `protectedProcedure`, imported at line 18 from `../../trpc`. `protectedProcedure` (apps/api/src/trpc.ts:208) only requires `ctx.user` to exist — it does not check role. The project's own `adminProcedure` export exists at apps/api/src/trpc.ts:256 (`t.procedure.use(isAuthed).use(isAdmin).use(tracingMiddleware)`) and `apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts:54-72` defines `createAdminMiddleware()` for exactly this purpose, but neither is used here.
findings.impact: Any authenticated user regardless of role (USER, SALES_REP, MANAGER — not just ADMIN as documented) can register a webhook source with an attacker-chosen `secret`/`signatureVerifier`/`allowedEvents`, or unregister (disable) an existing legitimate source such as a Stripe or SendGrid integration, contradicting the router's own 'admin only' comment and the RBAC model described in ADR-009.
findings.rootCause: Copy/paste of `protectedProcedure` instead of `adminProcedure` when the router was written; no test asserts a non-admin role is rejected.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-009-zero-trust-security.md (Role-Based Access Control decision driver: 'Support USER, SALES_REP, MANAGER, ADMIN roles' with least-privilege enforcement)
findings.remediation: Replace `protectedProcedure` with `adminProcedure` for both `registerSource` and `unregisterSource`. Add a router test asserting a `USER`/`SALES_REP` role receives `FORBIDDEN`. Note: exploitability may currently be reduced by SEC-009 (the webhook service is instantiated fresh per request rather than a shared singleton) — fix both together and re-verify.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 211-266: `export const inboundEmailRouter = createTRPCRouter({ /** Webhook endpoint for receiving inbound emails * This is a public endpoint that email providers call */ webhook: publicProcedure.input(InboundEmailWebhookSchema).mutation(async ({ input, ctx }) => { ... const { emailId, tenantId } = await storeEmail(parsed, ctx); ... }) })`. `InboundEmailWebhookSchema` (lines 43-65) validates shape only (all fields optional strings) — no HMAC/shared-secret/basic-auth check anywhere in this handler or in `storeEmail`/`resolveTenantForInboundEmail` (lines 1259-1315), which resolves the target tenant purely by matching the caller-supplied `to` address's domain against registered users' email domains and then persists the caller-supplied `from`, `subject`, `html`/`text` body as `status: 'DELIVERED'` (line 1349).
findings.impact: An unauthenticated external party who knows (or guesses) any tenant user's email domain can POST directly to this tRPC mutation and have IntelliFlow store an arbitrary, attacker-controlled 'received' email — with any `from` address, subject, and HTML body — as a genuine DELIVERED message in that tenant's inbox. This is usable for internal phishing (a forged email appearing to come from a colleague/vendor inside the CRM UI, rendered via `EmailMessage.tsx`'s DOMPurify-sanitized HTML — so not directly XSS, but a highly credible social-engineering vector) and for inbox-flooding since the only throttle is the generic `publicRateLimitMiddleware` (100 req/min per anonymous key, apps/api/src/middleware/rate-limit.ts:44).
findings.rootCause: SendGrid/Mailgun inbound-parse webhooks don't support standard HMAC signing out of the box, and no compensating control (shared secret query param, provider IP allowlist, or Basic Auth on the URL) was implemented to replace it.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-009-zero-trust-security.md ('never trust, always verify'); no specific ADR covers inbound email webhook auth, indicating a documentation gap as well as a code gap
findings.remediation: Add a shared-secret check (e.g. a `?token=` query param or `X-Inbound-Secret` header compared with `timingSafeEqual` against an env-configured secret, mirroring `packages/webhooks/src/framework.ts`'s `hmacSha256Verify` pattern) before processing the payload; alternatively verify the sending IP against the provider's published inbound-parse IP ranges. Track unauthenticated inbound volume in metrics/alerts. Add an ADR documenting the chosen verification mechanism.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 665-683: `private verifySignature(source, normalizedHeaders, rawBody): { verified: boolean } | { error: HandleResult } { const signature = normalizedHeaders[source.signatureHeader.toLowerCase()]; if (signature) { ... } if (source.secret) { return { error: { ..., message: 'Missing signature', statusCode: 401 } }; } return { verified: false }; }`. When a source is registered with an empty/falsy `secret` and the request has no signature header, the framework returns `{ verified: false }` (not an error) and `handle()` (line 596-598) proceeds to `dispatchEvent` regardless — handlers receive `context.verified === false` but the framework itself does not refuse to dispatch.
findings.impact: If a webhook source is ever registered without a secret (e.g. via SEC-003's broken RBAC, or simple misconfiguration), all events for that source are accepted and dispatched to handlers with no verification, and it is entirely up to each individual handler to remember to check `context.verified` — an easy step to miss. This is a 'secure by developer diligence' rather than 'secure by default' design.
findings.rootCause: The framework was designed to support both signed and unsigned/best-effort sources via the same code path, trading a secure default for flexibility.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-033-security-hardening.md (API protection / OWASP threat modeling intent)
findings.remediation: Require callers of `registerSource` to explicitly opt into `requireSignature: false`; default to refusing dispatch (401) when no secret/signature is present unless that flag is set. Audit `dispatchEvent` handlers to short-circuit when `context.verified === false` for sources that expect signing.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: line 61: `dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: renderedContent }}`. `renderedContent` comes from `parseMarkdown()` (lines 70-147), which HTML-escapes `<`/`>`/`&` at line 74 (blocking raw tag injection) but then re-inserts caller-supplied URLs verbatim into attribute context with no scheme allowlist: line 105 `html = html.replaceAll(/!\[([^\]]*)\]\(([^)]+)\)/g, '');` and lines 108-111 `html = html.replaceAll(/\[([^\]\[\r\n]+)\]\(([^)\r\n]+)\)/g, '$1');`. A markdown link `[click me](javascript:alert(document.cookie))` survives the escaping step (no `<`/`>` characters) and is rendered as ``.
findings.impact: Any content rendered through `MarkdownRenderer` (blog/content-authoring surfaces) executes attacker JS in the viewer's session on click if the markdown source is attacker-influenced (e.g. a lower-privileged author, or markdown ingested from an external source). Confirmed sibling component `tiptap-node-renderer.tsx` explicitly avoids `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` for exactly this reason ('the node tree is UNTRUSTED at render time, even though authoring is gated to ADMIN\MANAGER') — this component does not apply the same discipline.
findings.rootCause: Hand-rolled markdown-to-HTML converter reinserts capture-group URLs into attributes without a `javascript:`/`data:`/`vbscript:` scheme check; component docstring itself flags it as 'for demo purposes... In production, use a library like remark, marked, or react-markdown' (line 66-68), i.e. known-incomplete.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-033-security-hardening.md (AI/content output sanitization intent); general OWASP A03 Injection
findings.remediation: Either replace the hand-rolled parser with a maintained library (`marked`/`remark`) plus `DOMPurify.sanitize()` on the output (matching the pattern already used in `EmailMessage.tsx`), or add an explicit scheme allowlist (`/^(https?:|mailto:|#|\/)/i`) rejecting/neutralizing `href`/`src` values that don't match before interpolation.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 197-231: `async function spawnProcess(options: SpawnProcessOptions) { const { command, args, taskId, ... } = options; ... const proc = spawn(command, args, { cwd, env: {...}, shell: isWindows() }); ... }`. `shell: isWindows()` routes the call through `cmd.exe` on Windows, at which point `args` are subject to shell metacharacter interpretation (`&`, `|`, `;`, backticks are not meaningful in cmd.exe the same way, but `&`, `|`, `^` are) rather than being passed as an argv array directly to the child process. `apps/web/src/lib/test-runner/spawner.ts:155` has the identical pattern.
findings.impact: Any caller of `spawnProcess`/this spawner that builds `args` from user-controlled strings (e.g. a `taskId`, branch name, or free-text field) without its own allowlist validation is exposed to command injection on Windows hosts. One verified-safe caller was found (`apps/project-tracker/app/api/swarm/run-task/[taskId]/route.ts` validates `taskId` with `isValidTaskId()`/`sanitizeTaskId()` before use), but the spawner itself provides no such guarantee for other call sites and the safety currently depends entirely on every caller remembering to validate.
findings.rootCause: `shell: isWindows()` was added for 'better compatibility' (per inline comment) without a centralized argument-sanitization layer in the shared spawner; the guarantee lives in individual callers instead.
findings.remediation: Move argument validation into `spawnProcess` itself (e.g. reject args containing shell metacharacters, or avoid `shell: true` and resolve the executable path directly on Windows), so safety is not solely dependent on every caller independently sanitizing. Audit remaining callers of `subprocess-spawner.ts` and `spawner.ts` for any that pass unsanitized user input.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: This is an internal developer-tooling surface (project-tracker dashboard), not customer/tenant-facing, and the one call site inspected (run-task) does validate its input. Flagged as a hardening item because the shared primitive itself has no built-in guard, not because a live exploit path was confirmed; other callers were not individually audited given audit scope/time.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: packages/test-fixtures/access-policy.json:20-24: `"audit": { "enabled": false, "note": "Enable in production" }, "notes": ["DEV MODE: Using root token", "Policies defined but not applied"]`. Corroborated by infra/monitoring/vault-config.yaml:19-21 `auth: method: token # In production, use kubernetes or approle auth` and lines 150-157 `# NOTE: DEV MODE - For production use proper auth and TLS` / `Production checklist: [ ] Enable TLS ... [ ] Enable audit logging ...` (unchecked boxes). No secret VALUES are present in either file — only policy/config shape.
findings.impact: These are checked-in dev/test-fixture snapshots, not live infrastructure state, so there is no direct exposure from the files themselves. The risk is process, not code: the vault-config.yaml checklist items (TLS, auto-unseal, audit logging, backup/DR) are explicitly unchecked, and if the same 'dev mode: root token, audit disabled' posture were ever carried into a real Vault deployment, it would violate ADR-033's secrets-management driver and defeat SEC engineering's ability to detect secret-access misuse.
findings.rootCause: Dev-mode Vault artifacts (root token, disabled audit) were snapshotted into the repo for local development / test fixtures without a corresponding, verifiably-applied production config checked in alongside them.
findings.remediation: Confirm (out of band, e.g. with the platform/devops team) that production Vault has audit logging enabled, TLS configured, and non-root auth (kubernetes/approle/oidc) as the checklist requires, and record that confirmation as an attestation. Consider adding a CI check that fails if `vault-config.yaml`'s production section still contains unresolved `${VAR:-}` placeholders with empty defaults for required prod secrets.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: Files are explicitly labeled dev-mode/test-fixtures with a documented production checklist already present; this is process/verification hygiene rather than a confirmed vulnerability in the repo itself.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 54-73: `async function getWebhookService(ctx: Context) { const adapters = await import('@intelliflow/adapters'); const WebhookService = (adapters as any).WebhookService; ... // In production, retrieve from DI container // For now, create instance (stateless service) return new WebhookService(); }`. Every call to `registerSource`, `unregisterSource`, and `handleWebhook` invokes `getWebhookService(ctx)` independently and gets a brand-new `WebhookService` instance each time (line 87, 119, 149), rather than a shared/DI-resolved singleton.
findings.impact: If `WebhookService` internally wraps something like the in-memory `WebhookFramework` (packages/webhooks/src/framework.ts, whose `sources` map is instance-scoped), a source registered via `registerSource` in one request would not exist for a subsequent `handleWebhook` call, silently making the entire registration/verification feature non-functional. This lowers real-world exploitability of SEC-003/SEC-005 but is itself a correctness/security-configuration-drift risk: an operator could believe a webhook secret is enforced (because `registerSource` returned success) when it silently is not persisted, which is worse than an obvious failure.
findings.rootCause: TODO-style comment ('In production, retrieve from DI container') left unresolved; router was not wired into the DI container (`container.ts`) alongside the rest of the app per apps/api/CLAUDE.md's container-wiring requirement.
findings.remediation: Wire `WebhookService`/`WebhookFramework` into the DI container as a singleton (per apps/api/CLAUDE.md container.ts wiring pattern) so registered sources persist across requests, then re-verify SEC-003 and SEC-005 exploitability against the corrected, stateful service.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: Did not trace into packages/adapters' WebhookService implementation to confirm it is genuinely stateless per-instance (vs. wrapping a module-level singleton internally) — flagged from the router-level evidence and its own comment, not a confirmed runtime trace.
